title: Analysis of FrogID Data
text:
Data set: Australian frogs
This data set contains 2023 data from the app, FrogID. FrogID is an Australian app that allows people to submit frog calls and museum experts can identify the type of frog. Along with the FrogID data, there is an additional table that includes their names.
The frogID dataset contains 11 columns by 136621 rows. The dataset contains the following information:

occurrenceID
eventID
decimalLatitude
decimalLongitude
scientificName
eventDate
eventTime
timeZone
coordinateUncertaintyInMeters
recordedBy
stateProvince

The frog names dataset contains 5 columns and 294 rows. The dataset contains the following information:

subfamily
tribe
scientificName
commonName
secondary_commonNames

title: Analysis of FrogID Data
text:
Questions

What is the spatiotemporal distribution of Australian frog occurrences, and how does call frequency change across seasons?
Do geographic hotspots with the highest density of frog occurrences also exhibit the highest levels of taxonomic diversity?

title: Analysis of FrogID Data
text:
Analysis plan
Question 1: What is the spatiotemporal distribution of Australian frog occurrences, and how does call frequency change across seasons?

Due to Australia being in the southern hemisphere, their months for seasons are not the same as North America – Use eventDate to determine month and then season.

Fall: March, April, May
Winter: June, July, August
Spring: September, October, November
Summer: December, January, February

Make a map of Australia

Use decimalLatitude and decimalLongitude variables to plot where each of these calls are taking place in Australia. Additionally, map out the provinces using the stateProvince variable.

Map the provinces
Make four different plots for each season – in each plot is a map of Australia and its provinces with the density of frog occurrences perhaps using a heat map.



Question 2: Do geographic areas with the highest density of frog occurrences also exhibit the highest levels of taxonomic diversity?

Based off the previous data, we will be able to determine geographically where the most frog occurrences.
Determine what is considered the equatorial, tropical, subtropical, desert, grassland, temperate.

Make a visual of Australia defined by the different geographical regions equatorial, tropical, subtropical, desert, grassland, temperate.
Find a map online with regions and map it to preexisting map of Australia.

https://www.bom.gov.au/climate/maps/averages/climate-classification/?maptype=koppen_major_AGCDv2_&period=1991-2020


To determine taxonomic diversity, use the subfamily or tribe – see which one is better.

Plot the number of different subfamilies/tribes per geographical region.
Bar chart:

x-axis: region
y-axis: number of different subfamilies

Graph another plot that is the bar chart but stacked with the different subfamilies.
-   Try to map the provinces

-   Make four different plots for each season -- in each plot is a map of Australia and its provinces with the density of frog occurrences perhaps using a heat map

Do geographic areas with the highest density of frog occurrences also exhibit the highest levels of taxonomic diversity?

Based off the previous data, we will be able to determine geographically where the most frog occurrences.
Would have to be able to determine what is considered the equatorial, tropical, subtropical, desert, grassland, temperate

Make a visual of Australia defined by the different geographical regions equatorial, tropical, subtropical, desert, grassland, temperate.
Should be able to find a map online with regions and be able to map it
https://www.bom.gov.au/climate/maps/averages/climate-classification/?maptype=koppen_major_AGCDv2_&period=1991-2020

To determine taxonomic diversity, use the subfamily or tribe – see which one is better.

Figure out how to plot the number of different subfamilies/tribes per geographical region
Bar chart:

x-axis: region
y-axis: number of different subfamilies

Maybe another plot that is the bar chart but stacked with the different subfamilies

title: Project title
text:
Math Expressions
You can write LaTeX math expressions inside a pair of dollar signs, e.g. $\alpha+\beta$ renders \(\alpha + \beta\). You can use the display style with double dollar signs:
$$\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i$$
\[
\bar{X}=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^nX_i
\]
Limitations:

The source code of a LaTeX math expression must be in one line, unless it is inside a pair of double dollar signs, in which case the starting $$ must appear in the very beginning of a line, followed immediately by a non-space character, and the ending $$ must be at the end of a line, led by a non-space character;
There should not be spaces after the opening $ or before the closing $.

title: Project title
text:
Feeling adventurous?

You are welcomed to use the default styling of the slides. In fact, that’s what I expect majority of you will do. You will differentiate yourself with the content of your presentation.
But some of you might want to play around with slide styling. Some solutions for this can be found at https://quarto.org/docs/presentations/revealjs.

title: Analysis of FrogID Data
text:
Motivation
I majored in biology in undergraduate and really enjoyed the ecology electives I took. Additionally, I love watching nature and animal documentaries so choosing a frog data set seemed fitting and peaked my interest.
